elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we propose a new well-balanced fifth-order finite volume WENO method for solving one- and two-dimensional shallow water equations with bottom topography.
elements.sent-2411-003: The well-balanced property is crucial to the ability of a scheme to simulate perturbation waves over the ``lake-at-rest'' steady state such as waves on a lake or tsunami waves in the deep ocean.
elements.sent-2411-004: We adopt the constant subtraction technique such that both the flux gradient and source term in the new pre-balanced form vanish at the lake-at-rest steady state, while the well-balanced WENO method by Xing and Shu [Commun.. Comput.
elements.sent-2411-005: Phys., 2006] uses high-order accurate numerical discretization of the source term and makes the exact balance between the source term and the flux gradient, to achieve the well-balanced property.
elements.sent-2411-006: The scaling positivity-preserving limiter is used for the water height near the dry areas.
elements.sent-2411-007: The fifth-order WENO-AO reconstruction is used to construct the solution since it has better resolution than the WENO-ZQ and WENO-MR reconstructions for the perturbation of steady state flows.
elements.sent-2411-008: Extensive one- and two-dimensional numerical examples are presented to demonstrate the well-balanced, fifth-order accuracy, non-oscillatory, and positivity-preserving properties of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-009: Keywords: Well-balanced; WENO; Shallow water equations; Constant subtraction technique
elements.sent-2411-010: The shallow water equations (SWEs) with source terms play a critical role in the modeling and simulation of flows in rivers and coastal areas.
elements.sent-2411-011: They have wide applications in ocean and hydraulic engineering, such as tidal flows in estuary and coastal water regions, bore wave propagation, rivers, reservoirs, and open channel flows, etc. { The one-dimensional (1D) SWEs take the original form \begin{equation}\label{swe-1d} \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \begin{bmatrix*}[c] h hu \end{bmatrix*} +\frac{\partial}{\partial x} \begin{bmatrix*}[c] hu hu^2+\ha gh^2 \end{bmatrix*} = \begin{bmatrix*}[c] 0 -ghb_x \end{bmatrix*},\end{equation} where $h=h(x,t)\geq0$ is the water height, $u$ is the water velocity, $b=b(x)$ represents the bottom topography, and $g$ is the gravitational constant.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{swe-1d} \frac{\partial}{\partial t} [c] h hu  +\frac{\partial}{\partial x} [c] hu hu^2+\ha gh^2  = [c] 0 -ghb_x ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-012: There are some challenges in developing robust and accurate numerical methods for the system \eqref{swe-1d .}
elements.sent-2411-013: One is that the system produces both smooth and non-smooth solutions (e.g., the hydraulic jumps/shock waves, rarefaction waves) even when the initial date is smooth.
elements.sent-2411-014: Second, we need to ensure the nonnegativity of the water height near the dry situation.
elements.sent-2411-015: Third is that the system admits steady state solutions in which the flux gradient is balanced by the source term exactly.
elements.sent-2411-016: An important steady state solution is the so-called ``lake-at-rest": \be h+b=C, \quad hu=0.
elements.sent-2411-017: A significant result in computing such solutions was given by Bermudez and Vazquez \cite{bermudez1994upwind in $1994$.}
elements.sent-2411-018: They proposed the idea of the exact ``C-property'', which refers to the ability of the scheme to exactly preserve the lake-at-rest steady state solution \eqref{steady state .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Such numerical methods are often regarded as well-balanced methods.
elements.sent-2411-020: Since then, many well-balanced methods have been developed to approximate the SWEs.
elements.sent-2411-021: LeVeque \cite{leveque1998balancing introduced a quasi-steady wave-propagation algorithm by designing a Riemann problem in the center of each grid cell such that the flux difference exactly cancels the source term.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Zhou et al. \cite{zhou2001SR proposed a surface gradient method for the treatment of the source terms based on an accurate reconstruction of the conservative variables at cell interfaces.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Audusse et al. \cite{audusse2004fast introduced the concept of hydrostatic reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Tang et al. \cite{tang2004gas extended a kinetic flux vector splitting scheme to solve the SWEs with source terms.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Chen et al.~\cite{chen2022unified presented a second-order accurate bottom-surface-gradient method.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Vukovic and Sopta \cite{vukovic2002eno proposed the well-balanced finite difference ENO and WENO schemes with the decomposed source term, where the ENO and WENO reconstruction are applied to both the flux and the source term.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Li et al. \cite{li2014high developed a well-balanced fourth-order central WENO method for the one-dimensional SWEs.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Recently, Wang et al. \cite{WANG20201387 presented a fifth-order finite difference well-balanced multi-resolution WENO method.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Zhao and Zhang \cite{zhao2023well proposed a well-balanced fifth-order finite difference Hermite WENO method.}
elements.sent-2411-030: More related well-balanced high-order methods include finite difference methods \cite{huang2022high,li2012hybrid,xing2005high,xing2006high,zhang2023high,zhu2016new , finite volume methods \cite{chen2017new,chen2022unified,noelle2007high,qian2022positivity,zhang2023structure , and DG methods \cite{bokhove2005flooding,bunya2009wetting,du2019well,xing2010positivity, zhang2021high, CiCP-31-94,zhang2010positivity .}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: We consider the well-balanced fifth-order finite volume WENO-AO scheme with the constant subtraction technique (CST) for SWEs in this paper, denoted as WENOAO-CST.
elements.sent-2411-032: The WENO-AO reconstruction \cite{BALSARA2016780 is used to construct the solution at the cell interface since the WENO-AO reconstruction has better resolution than the fifth-order WENO-ZQ and WENO-MR reconstructions for the simulation of small perturbation (cf.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The CST is adopted to achieve the well-balanced property of the method, such that both the flux gradient and source term { in the new pre-balanced form (denoted as the `` CST pre-balanced form") vanish at the lake-at-rest steady state.}
elements.sent-2411-035: The CST was first proposed in \cite{yang2015well to construct well-balanced central schemes for SWEs.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Then, Du et al. \cite{du2019well extended the CST to construct a well-balanced DG method for SWEs on unstructured meshes.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In this paper, we further explore the potential of the CST to develop a high-order well-balanced finite volume WENO method for SWEs.
elements.sent-2411-038: Compared to the well-balanced WENO method by Xing and Shu (denoted as WENOJS-XS) \cite{xing2006new , the well-balanced property of our WENOAO-CST method is automatically achieved since { {not only the flux gradient is balanced by the source term in the CST pre-balanced form (cf.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: However, the WENOJS-XS method uses high-order accurate numerical discretization of the source terms, which mimics the approximation of the flux term, so that the exact balance between the source term and the flux gradient can be achieved numerically at the steady state.
elements.sent-2411-040: To be specific, the WENOJS-XS method needs to use the same non-linear WENO weights computed from $h$ on $b$ to obtain corresponding reconstructions of $b$, and the numerical integration of the source term must be computed exactly by using a suitable Gauss quadrature rule.
elements.sent-2411-041: It is worth pointing out that, unlike the WENOJS-XS method, the numerical integration { {for the source term of the CST pre-balanced form does not need to be computed exactly in the WENOAO-CST method, and we only need to use the numerical quadrature rule for the source term to satisfy the fifth-order accuracy.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Thus, it is obvious that the procedure of the WENOAO-CST method is better implemented and the format becomes easier than the WENOJS-XS.
elements.sent-2411-043: In addition, the WENOAO-CST method is more efficient than the WENOJS-XS method in the sense that the former leads to a smaller error than the latter for a fixed amount of CPU time (cf.
elements.sent-2411-044: Example \ref{accuracy test and Example \ref{accuracy test-2D in \S\ref{num_sec ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The organization of the paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-046: A well-balanced and positivity-preserving fifth-order finite volume WENOAO-CST method for the one-dimensional SWEs is developed in \S\ref{WENO_CST_1D , and the extension to two dimensions in a dimension-by-dimension manner is described in \S\ref{sec_2D .}}
elements.sent-2411-047: One- and two-dimensional numerical results are presented in \S\ref{num_sec to show the accuracy, high resolution, well-balanced, and positivity-preserving properties of the proposed WENOAO-CST method.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Finally, the conclusions and remarks are given in \S\ref{sec_con .}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Preservation of positivity for water height $h$
elements.sent-2411-084: Another challenge in the numerical simulation for the SWEs is to preserve the nonnegativity of the water height $h$ in the computation.
elements.sent-2411-085: The dry area often emerges in the simulation of dam breaks, flood wave flows, and run-up phenomena over shores and sea defense structures.
elements.sent-2411-086: Standard numerical methods may fail around the dry area and result in non-physical negative water height.
elements.sent-2411-087: In this section, we show that the WENOAO-CST method with the scaling PP limiter can preserve the non-negativity of the water height $h$.

elements.sent-2411-234: In this paper, a fifth-order well-balanced finite volume WENOAO-CST method is developed to solve one- and two-dimensional SWEs with flat and non-flat bottom topographies { in the CST pre-balanced form .}
elements.sent-2411-235: The WENO-AO approach is used in the reconstruction to ensure the high-order accuracy and non-oscillatory properties of the method.
elements.sent-2411-236: Compared with other WENO reconstruction schemes( e.g., WENO-ZQ and WENO-MR reconstructions), the WENO-AO reconstruction has better resolution for some numerical examples.
elements.sent-2411-237: Furthermore, a PP limiter is adopted for the computation of solutions with dry areas.
elements.sent-2411-238: The proposed WENOAO-CST method outperforms the WENOJS-XS method \cite{xing2011high in terms of accuracy and efficiency verified by the numerical results.}

elements.section-2411-009: Appendix B.\ The proof of Proposition \ref{prop_ahpha_1D}
elements.sent-2411-248: In this section, we give a proof of Proposition \ref{prop_ahpha_1D based on the idea of \cite{xing2011high,xing2010positivity .}}
elements.sent-2411-249: We start by showing the following lemma on the positivity of a first-order Lax-Friedrichs scheme with a well-balanced flux.
elements.sent-2411-250: Consider the first-order scheme with the Lax-Friedrichs flux \begin{equation}{h}_{i}^{n+1} ={h}_{i}^{n} -\lambda\left(\hat{\bF}^h\left(h_{i}^{*,+},u_{i}^n; h_{i+1}^{*,-},u_{i+1}^n\right)-\hat{\bF}^h\left(h_{i-1}^{*,+},u_{i-1}^n; h_{i}^{*,-},u_{i}^n\right)\right),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}h^{*,+}_{i}=\max(0,h^n_{i}+b_{i}-\max(b_{i},b_{i+1})), h^{*,-}_{i}=\max(0,h^n_{i}+b_{i}-\max(b_{i-1},b_{i})).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-251: If ${h _{i ^{n ,~h_{i\pm1 ^n$ are non-negative, then ${h _{i ^{n+1}$ is also non-negative under the CFL condition $\lambda\alpha\leqslant1$, with $\alpha=\max\limits_i(|u_i^n|+\sqrt{gh_i^n )$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-252: \begin{equation}{h}_{i}^{n+1} ={h}_{i}^{n}-\frac{\lambda}{2}\left(h_i^{*,+}u_i^n+h_{i+1}^{*,-}u_{i+1}^n- \alpha(h_{i+1}^{*,-}-h^{*,+}_i)\right) \quad \quad \,\, +\frac{\lambda}{2}\left(h_{i-1}^{*,+}u_{i-1}^n+h_{i}^{*,-}u_{i}^n- \alpha(h_{i}^{*,-}-h^{*,+}_{i-1})\right) =\left(1-\frac{\lambda}{2}(\alpha+u_i^n)\frac{h_i^{*,+}}{h_i^n} -\frac{\lambda}{2}(\alpha-u_i^n)\frac{h_i^{*,-}}{h_i^n}\right)h_i^n \quad \, +\left[\frac{\lambda}{2}(\alpha+u_{i-1}^n)\frac{h_{i-1}^{*,+}}{h_{i-1}^n}\right]h_{i-1}^n +\left[\frac{\lambda}{2}(\alpha-u_{i+1}^n)\frac{h_{i+1}^{*,-}}{h_{i+1}^n}\right]h_{i+1}^n\end{equation} One can see that ${h _{i ^{n+1}$ is a linear combination of ${h _{i ^{n ,~h_{i-1 ^n$ and $h_{i+1 ^n$ and all the coefficients are non-negative.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-254: Now, we provide a complete proof of Proposition \ref{prop_ahpha_1D .}
